beats.text: A tensor is PyTorch's core data structure: an n-dimensional array of one dtype in a contiguous block of memory.

visual.sub: shape, stride, and one device switch

beats.text: Every tensor carries a shape and a stride; the flat memory address of an element is a dot product of its index with the stride vector.

visual.note: For a contiguous 2x3 tensor strides are (3,1); element (1,2) sits at offset 5.

beats.text: Views like transpose and reshape share storage and only reinterpret strides, so reshaping a billion-element tensor copies nothing.
Broadcasting aligns shapes from the right; a pair of axes is compatible if they are equal or one of them is 1.

visual.note: (64,1) with (768,) gives (64,768); (3,) with (4,) is an error.

beats.text: Move the same tensor to a GPU with x.to('cuda') and every operation dispatches to a CUDA kernel; the math is identical, only the silicon changes.
One honest caveat: PyTorch defaults to float32 where NumPy uses float64, and CPU and GPU reductions can differ bit-for-bit.
Setting requires_grad on a tensor makes PyTorch record every operation that touches it into a directed acyclic graph.
The graph is dynamic: it is built during the forward pass and torn down after backward, so an if or a loop produces a different graph each iteration.
Calling loss.backward() walks the graph in reverse, applying the chain rule, and the adjoint of a node sums contributions from every child it feeds.

visual.note: A tensor that fans out to several children has its gradient summed over all paths.

beats.text: On the graph c = a*b, d = c + a, L = d squared at a=2, b=3, the leaf a feeds two children so its gradient lands at 64.
Reverse-mode autodiff gets the gradient of one scalar loss with respect to all parameters in a single backward pass.
Two traps: .grad accumulates across backward calls so you must zero it, and only leaf tensors keep a gradient by default.
nn.Module is a base class that bookkeeps parameters: subclass it, register child modules in __init__, and define forward.
module.parameters() recursively yields every learnable tensor, which is exactly what you hand to the optimizer.
A single nn.Linear holds a weight of shape d_out by d_in and a bias of shape d_out, computing a learned affine map.

visual.tex: y = x W^{\top} + b, \quad W \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}} \times d_{\text{in}}}, \; b \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{out}}}
visual.note: Parameter count per layer is d_out times (d_in + 1); the +1 is the bias.

beats.text: The two-layer MLP Linear(784,128) then Linear(128,10) carries 101,770 learnable parameters; ReLU adds none.
Total parameter count for the MLP, summing both linear layers with their biases.
Use nn.Parameter, not a plain tensor, or the optimizer never sees it; that is a classic loss-won't-go-down bug.
Everything converges on five lines you write yourself; PyTorch deliberately does not hide the loop, which is why it is easy to debug.

visual.sub: five lines, run millions of times

beats.text: The order is load-bearing: zero_grad, then forward, then loss, then backward, then step.
The optimizer step is where learning happens; for plain SGD it subtracts the learning rate times the gradient.

visual.note: step() walks every parameter and consumes the .grad values backward() just deposited.

beats.text: A parameter at 2.0 with gradient 3.0 and learning rate 0.2 moves to 1.4 after one optimizer step.
Run the loop on a noisy line and the mean squared error loss falls steeply over the first epochs.
Forget zero_grad and gradients sum across iterations; call step before backward and you update on stale gradients.
A Dataset answers two questions: how many examples it has, and how to return example i as a features-and-label tuple.
A DataLoader wraps a Dataset and handles batching, shuffling each epoch, and parallel loading via worker subprocesses.
Steps per epoch is the ceiling of N over B; the loader yields that many minibatches and that many optimizer steps.

visual.note: Total optimizer steps equal epochs times ceil(N/B), the number that sets your training budget.

beats.text: With 10,000 examples and batch size 128, one epoch takes 79 optimizer steps; the last batch holds the remaining 16.
Device discipline means a tensor and the parameters it meets must live on the same device, or PyTorch raises an error.
From here torch.compile, mixed precision, and DistributedDataParallel scale the same loop without changing the four core ideas.
